does: This paper investigates how large language models behave during multi-turn deliberation on moral questions, moving beyond single-turn evaluations to examine realistic interactive settings. The authors conduct a study where subsets of three models (GPT-4.1, Claude 3.7 Sonnet, and Gemini 2.0 Flash) debate and collectively assign blame in 1,000 everyday moral dilemmas from Reddit's Am I the Asshole community. They employ two deliberation structures—synchronous (parallel responses) and round-robin (sequential responses)—to test how interaction protocols and order effects influence the models' moral reasoning and whether they revise their verdicts through debate. The core contribution is empirical evidence demonstrating striking behavioral differences across these conditions, revealing that value alignment in LLMs is highly dependent on deliberative dynamics and interaction structure rather than being stable across contexts.
connects: This work builds on growing research examining value alignment and moral reasoning in LLMs, extending beyond the dominant paradigm of single-turn prompt evaluations. It responds to recent work on multi-agent LLM systems and debate-based approaches for improving reasoning and factuality, while engaging with broader questions about how coordination protocols shape emergent behavior in AI systems. The paper also connects to research on order effects and preference instability in language models, and draws on methodologies from AI safety work investigating whether current evaluation approaches adequately capture model behavior in realistic deployment scenarios. It reflects increasing attention to the gap between controlled benchmarks and actual interactive use cases where LLMs provide sensitive guidance on moral and personal matters.
enables: This research opens pathways for designing more robust moral reasoning systems by showing that deliberation structure fundamentally shapes LLM outputs on value-laden questions. It suggests future work on optimizing interaction protocols for sensitive decision-making domains and designing debate formats that promote more consistent, defensible moral reasoning. The findings could enable development of better oversight mechanisms and verification procedures for LLM-assisted guidance in healthcare, counseling, and ethical decision support. More broadly, it enables research into whether certain interaction structures are inherently better for value alignment, how to detect and mitigate problematic order effects, and whether multi-agent debate can be harnessed to improve rather than merely expose misalignment. This work also points toward investigating which deliberation formats best capture and preserve genuine model capabilities versus artifacts of interaction design.
